text: Seneca distinguishes three times in Letter I: the past, which is fixed and therefore safe; the present, which is ours if we seize it; and the future, which is uncertain and not yet ours to spend. His advice is counterintuitive — look back often. The past is the only time we cannot lose, because it is already secured. A life well examined accumulates what he calls a 'treasury of memory,' and reviewing it is not nostalgia but inventory. The person who cannot recall what they did last Tuesday has functionally lost that day twice: once in living it poorly, once in failing to reclaim it through reflection.
question: Why does Seneca call the past the only time we cannot lose?

options.text: Because memory of suffering prevents repeating the same mistakes
Because it is already fixed and secured beyond anyone's reach
Because the future is too uncertain to spend any thought on
Because Stoics valued tradition more than present experience
